title: Neurological Emergencies
learningObjectives: Recognize Cushing's triad and its significance for increased intracranial pressure.
Apply the FAST stroke assessment mnemonic.
Explain the importance of a neurological exam in a patient with headache.
Recognize meningitis's classic triad of findings.
Distinguish stroke-mimicking presentations, especially diabetic emergencies.

summary.title: Cushing's Triad: A Sign of Rising Intracranial Pressure
summary.content: Cushing's triad — hypertension, bradycardia, and an altered respiratory pattern — is a late but ominous sign of significantly increased intracranial pressure. It reflects the brainstem's response to being compressed as pressure inside the skull rises, often from bleeding, swelling, or a mass effect. Recognizing this triad means recognizing a neurological emergency that may need rapid neurosurgical intervention; by the time it fully appears, the pressure elevation is already severe.

summary.title: FAST: Rapid Stroke Recognition
summary.content: Stroke assessment protocols commonly use FAST — Face (drooping), Arm (drift or weakness), Speech (slurred or difficulty), Time (note last known well time) — as a rapid field screening tool. The 'Time' component is as important as the physical findings, because eligibility for time-sensitive stroke treatments depends on precisely knowing when the patient was last seen normal, not just when symptoms were discovered.

summary.title: The Neurological Exam in Headache
summary.content: When assessing a patient with a headache, a neurological exam should be performed to rule out more serious causes — a headache alone is common and usually benign, but red flags such as the 'worst headache of my life,' new focal neurological deficits, altered mental status, fever with neck stiffness, or a headache with a Cushing's-triad pattern change the calculus entirely. The exam is what separates a benign headache from a stroke, bleed, or infection presenting as head pain.

summary.title: Meningitis: Fever, Nuchal Rigidity, and Altered Mentation
summary.content: Meningitis is an infection and is associated with fever, nuchal rigidity (neck stiffness), and altered mental status. Because it is an infectious, potentially rapidly progressive condition, recognizing this pattern — rather than dismissing it as a simple headache or viral illness — is important for early treatment and, when suspected, appropriate provider protection with standard/droplet precautions given possible bacterial causes.

summary.title: Stroke Mimics: Diabetic Emergencies
summary.content: Diabetic emergencies (hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia) can present with stroke-like symptoms — unilateral weakness, slurred speech, or altered mental status that closely mimics an acute stroke. Checking blood glucose is therefore a mandatory, rapid step in any suspected stroke presentation, since correcting a low glucose can fully reverse the neurological deficit in minutes, while a true stroke would not respond to glucose administration at all.

clinicalPearls: Cushing's Triad includes hypertension, bradycardia, and an altered respiratory pattern — indicating significantly elevated intracranial pressure.
Review the stroke assessment protocol (e.g., FAST: Face, Arm, Speech, Time).
When assessing a patient with a headache, perform a neurological exam to rule out serious causes.
Meningitis is associated with fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status.
Diabetic emergencies can present with stroke-like symptoms — always check glucose.
redFlags: Hypertension with bradycardia and an irregular respiratory pattern together — Cushing's triad, a neurosurgical emergency.
A headache described as the 'worst of my life' or with new focal deficits.
Fever, neck stiffness, and altered mental status together — think meningitis.
commonMistakes: Treating stroke-like symptoms without first checking glucose, missing a reversible hypoglycemic mimic.
Dismissing a severe or unusual headache without a neurological exam.
Missing the 'Time' component of FAST by not establishing a precise last-known-well time.
memoryAids: Cushing's Triad: high BP, low HR, irregular breathing — pressure is rising.
Headache + fever + stiff neck = think meningitis.
Glucose first in every 'stroke' call — hypoglycemia is the great stroke mimic.

decisionTrees.title: Neurological Emergency Assessment
decisionTrees.content:
Altered neurological presentation
  → Check glucose FIRST if any stroke-like symptoms (rule out hypo/hyperglycemia mimic)
  → Apply FAST: Face, Arm, Speech, Time (last known well)
  → Vitals show hypertension + bradycardia + abnormal respiratory pattern? → Cushing's Triad — suspect elevated ICP, urgent transport
  → Headache present? → Perform full neuro exam; red flags (worst-ever, focal deficit, fever+stiff neck) escalate concern
  → Fever + nuchal rigidity + altered mental status? → Suspect meningitis — precautions + rapid transport

references: EMPACT clinical pearls collection — Neurology category
